narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 69% chance that you would not pass. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the university maintain strong bar passage rates for its graduates. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your academic decile rankings from earlier years in school—specifically, your position in the lowest decile for Year 3 grades stood out as the biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, far below the typical mid-range deciles seen among students who passed the bar. Your Year 1 decile in the lowest group also contributed notably in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about early academic performance compared to passers who averaged around the sixth decile. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.50 worked in your favor, as it's higher than the 3.24 average among those who passed, helping to pull the prediction somewhat toward success. While your LSAT score of 27.50 is well below the 37.27 average for bar passers and your low family income bracket is uncommon among successful students (where middle and upper-middle incomes dominate), these played supporting roles rather than leading ones. Your choice to attend full-time aligns well with the vast majority of passers, but it wasn't enough to offset the key academic signals. Overall, your profile compares to accepted students by showing strengths in recent undergrad work but notable gaps in school deciles and standardized testing that mirrored patterns in past bar failures. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven flag based on historical trends. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, strengthening your early-grade performance through additional coursework or explanations in a new application, and exploring programs with holistic reviews that weigh your UGPA advantage more heavily. You might also seek mentorship from bar-passed alumni in similar circumstances to build a stronger case next time. With targeted improvements, your odds could shift dramatically in future cycles.
